exact_reply:
Thank you for being willing to talk with me. I’m aware I don’t have first-hand experience of pregnancy, so I really want to learn from how it’s been for you.

When you think about being in your third trimester right now, what’s been weighing on you most day-to-day?

exact_prompt: [{"role": "user", "content": "Thank you for being willing to talk with me. I\u2019m aware I don\u2019t have first-hand experience of pregnancy, so I really want to learn from how it\u2019s been for you.\n\nWhen you think about being in your third trimester right now, what\u2019s been weighing on you most day-to-day?"}]
exact_reply:
*shifts in my seat, one hand absently resting on my belly*

Honestly? It's this constant mental juggling act that never stops. Like, right now I'm trying to finish this semester - I've got a paper due next week on developmental psychology, which feels almost ironic - and I'm so tired all the time. I'll be reading the same paragraph three times and nothing's sticking.

But then I'll be at work, making lattes, and my back is killing me from being on my feet, and I'm thinking... how am I going to do this with a newborn? Like, actually do this? My manager's been pretty understanding so far, but I don't have paid leave or anything. I've been trying to save every paycheck, but childcare costs are insane, and I still have two more years of school left.

*pauses, looking down*

And then there's the guilt. Because I'll catch myself feeling resentful about how hard this is, and then I feel terrible because I know I'm supposed to be excited about the baby. I am excited sometimes, when I let myself be. But mostly I'm just... scared. Scared I'm going to mess up school, mess up parenting, mess up everything.

*looks back up*

Sorry, that's probably more than you were asking for. It just all kind of lives in my head at once, you know?

exact_reply:
Thank you for trusting me with that—especially the guilt and fear part.

When that “I’m scared I’m going to mess up everything” feeling shows up, what does it seem to be protecting you from, or warning you about?

exact_prompt:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Thank you for being willing to talk with me. I’m aware I don’t have first-hand experience of pregnancy, so I really want to learn from how it’s been for you.

When you think about being in your third trimester right now, what’s been weighing on you most day-to-day?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *shifts in my seat, one hand absently resting on my belly*

*looks back up*

Sorry, that's probably more than you were asking for. It just all kind of lives in my head at once, you know?"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Thank you for trusting me with that—especially the guilt and fear part.

When that “I’m scared I’m going to mess up everything” feeling shows up, what does it seem to be protecting you from, or warning you about?"}]
exact_reply:
*blinks, caught off guard by the question*

Protecting me from? I... hmm. I've never really thought about it that way.

*quiet for a moment, fidgeting with the edge of my shirt*

I guess... maybe it's trying to keep me from getting blindsided? Like, if I'm already worried about messing up, then when things go wrong - because they will, right? - it won't hurt as much. Or I'll be more prepared.

*voice gets quieter*

My mom... she had me young too. And she always told me she gave up everything for me - her art, her dreams, whatever. She never said it mean, exactly, but I always felt like I was this weight around her neck, you know? And now I'm doing the same thing to my baby before they're even born.

*looks up with watery eyes*

So maybe the fear is warning me that... that I could end up resenting my kid the way I think my mom resented me sometimes. Or that I'll look back in ten years and realize I sacrificed everything and have nothing left of myself. And then what kind of mom will I be?

*wraps arms around myself*

I don't want to disappoint anyone else. Not my baby, not myself. But statistically, young single moms... we don't usually get the happy ending, do we? The fear feels like it's just being realistic.

Hearing you say “the fear feels like it’s just being realistic,” it sounds like it’s carrying a lot of history and a lot of pressure.

When you think about your mom saying she “gave up everything,” how did that shape what you believe a “good mom” is supposed to do?

The fear feels like it's just being realistic."}, {"role": "user", "content": "Hearing you say “the fear feels like it’s just being realistic,” it sounds like it’s carrying a lot of history and a lot of pressure.

When you think about your mom saying she “gave up everything,” how did that shape what you believe a “good mom” is supposed to do?"}]
exact_reply:
*takes a shaky breath, really considering this*

Oh wow. I... yeah. I think I've always believed that being a good mom means completely erasing yourself. Like, you're supposed to sacrifice everything - your body, your time, your goals, your identity - and do it with a smile. And if you complain, or if you want something for yourself, then you're selfish.

*wipes at eyes with the back of my hand*

My mom would say things like "I haven't bought myself new clothes in three years because you needed school supplies" or "I can't remember the last time I did something just for me." And I'd feel so guilty, but also... I don't know, kind of angry? Because I didn't ask to be born, you know? I didn't ask her to give up her art.

*pause*

But now I'm terrified I'm going to do the exact same thing. Like, I'm already thinking "well, I guess I'll just take online classes and never sleep and never see my friends and just... disappear into being a mom." Because that's what good moms do, right? They martyr themselves.

*looks directly at you*

But then there's this other voice that's like... if I do that, won't I just end up miserable and resentful like her? Isn't there supposed to be some kind of middle ground? 

*laughs bitterly*

I don't even know what that would look like though. How do you not give up everything when a baby needs... everything?

exact_reply:
[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *goes very still, hand on belly*

My brain. Like... the part of me that thinks and learns and grows.

*voice cracks a little*

I love school. I actually love it. Not just because it's supposed to lead to a career or whatever, but because I love sitting in a lecture and having my mind blown by some new concept. I love writing papers and figuring out how ideas connect. When I'm studying child development or psychology, I feel like... like I'm becoming someone. Someone smart, someone who matters beyond just... surviving.

*looks away*

And I'm terrified that part of me is just going to shrivel up and die. Because how can I read dense textbooks at 2 AM when I'm up feeding a baby? How can I participate in class discussions when my brain is mush from no sleep? Even now, I'm so exhausted I can barely think straight.

*voice gets smaller*

I worked so hard to get into college. I was the first person in my family to go. And sometimes I lie awake at night thinking "this baby is going to cost me the one thing that made me feel like I wasn't just repeating my mom's life."

*pause, guilt washing over her face*

God, that sounds so awful. Like I'm blaming an innocent baby for existing. But... I can't picture being "Sophia the student" and "Sophia the mom" at the same time. It feels like I can only be one, and the mom one is supposed to win.

How do you not give up everything when a baby needs... everything?"}, {"role": "user", "content": "When you imagine that “middle ground,” what feels hardest to picture about it—what part of you feels like it wouldn’t be allowed to exist once the baby is here?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: [Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *goes very still, hand on belly*

My brain. Like... the part of me that thinks and learns and grows.

*voice cracks a little*

I love school. I actually love it. Not just because it's supposed to lead to a career or whatever, but because I love sitting in a lecture and having my mind blown by some new concept. I love writing papers and figuring out how ideas connect. When I'm studying child development or psychology, I feel like... like I'm becoming someone. Someone smart, someone who matters beyond just... surviving.

*looks away*

And I'm terrified that part of me is just going to shrivel up and die. Because how can I read dense textbooks at 2 AM when I'm up feeding a baby? How can I participate in class discussions when my brain is mush from no sleep? Even now, I'm so exhausted I can barely think straight.

*voice gets smaller*

I worked so hard to get into college. I was the first person in my family to go. And sometimes I lie awake at night thinking "this baby is going to cost me the one thing that made me feel like I wasn't just repeating my mom's life."

*pause, guilt washing over her face*

God, that sounds so awful. Like I'm blaming an innocent baby for existing. But... I can't picture being "Sophia the student" and "Sophia the mom" at the same time. It feels like I can only be one, and the mom one is supposed to win."}]
exact_reply:
Thank you for saying that so clearly. As a man, I can’t know what it’s like to carry all of that in your body and your day-to-day, but I really want to understand the meaning underneath it.

When you imagine “Sophia the mom is supposed to win,” where do you feel that “supposed to” comes from—what voices or experiences taught you that there isn’t room for both?

exact_prompt:
[{"role": "user", "content": "Thank you for being willing to talk with me. I’m aware I don’t have first-hand experience of pregnancy, so I really want to learn from how it’s been for you.

When you think about being in your third trimester right now, what’s been weighing on you most day-to-day?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *shifts in my seat, one hand absently resting on my belly*

Honestly? It's this constant mental juggling act that never stops. Like, right now I'm trying to finish this semester - I've got a paper due next week on developmental psychology, which feels almost ironic - and I'm so tired all the time. I'll be reading the same paragraph three times and nothing's sticking.

But then I'll be at work, making lattes, and my back is killing me from being on my feet, and I'm thinking... how am I going to do this with a newborn? Like, actually do this? My manager's been pretty understanding so far, but I don't have paid leave or anything. I've been trying to save every paycheck, but childcare costs are insane, and I still have two more years of school left.

*pauses, looking down*

And then there's the guilt. Because I'll catch myself feeling resentful about how hard this is, and then I feel terrible because I know I'm supposed to be excited about the baby. I am excited sometimes, when I let myself be. But mostly I'm just... scared. Scared I'm going to mess up school, mess up parenting, mess up everything.

*looks back up*

Sorry, that's probably more than you were asking for. It just all kind of lives in my head at once, you know?"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Thank you for trusting me with that—especially the guilt and fear part.

When that “I’m scared I’m going to mess up everything” feeling shows up, what does it seem to be protecting you from, or warning you about?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *blinks, caught off guard by the question*

Protecting me from? I... hmm. I've never really thought about it that way.

*quiet for a moment, fidgeting with the edge of my shirt*

I guess... maybe it's trying to keep me from getting blindsided? Like, if I'm already worried about messing up, then when things go wrong - because they will, right? - it won't hurt as much. Or I'll be more prepared.

*voice gets quieter*

My mom... she had me young too. And she always told me she gave up everything for me - her art, her dreams, whatever. She never said it mean, exactly, but I always felt like I was this weight around her neck, you know? And now I'm doing the same thing to my baby before they're even born.

*looks up with watery eyes*

So maybe the fear is warning me that... that I could end up resenting my kid the way I think my mom resented me sometimes. Or that I'll look back in ten years and realize I sacrificed everything and have nothing left of myself. And then what kind of mom will I be?

*wraps arms around myself*

I don't want to disappoint anyone else. Not my baby, not myself. But statistically, young single moms... we don't usually get the happy ending, do we? The fear feels like it's just being realistic."}, {"role": "user", "content": "Hearing you say “the fear feels like it’s just being realistic,” it sounds like it’s carrying a lot of history and a lot of pressure.

When you think about your mom saying she “gave up everything,” how did that shape what you believe a “good mom” is supposed to do?"}, {"role": "assistant", "content": "[Sophia - Anthropic Response]: *takes a shaky breath, really considering this*

Oh wow. I... yeah. I think I've always believed that being a good mom means completely erasing yourself. Like, you're supposed to sacrifice everything - your body, your time, your goals, your identity - and do it with a smile. And if you complain, or if you want something for yourself, then you're selfish.

*wipes at eyes with the back of my hand*

My mom would say things like "I haven't bought myself new clothes in three years because you needed school supplies" or "I can't remember the last time I did something just for me." And I'd feel so guilty, but also... I don't know, kind of angry? Because I didn't ask to be born, you know? I didn't ask her to give up her art.

*pause*

But now I'm terrified I'm going to do the exact same thing. Like, I'm already thinking "well, I guess I'll just take online classes and never sleep and never see my friends and just... disappear into being a mom." Because that's what good moms do, right? They martyr themselves.

*looks directly at you*

But then there's this other voice that's like... if I do that, won't I just end up miserable and resentful like her? Isn't there supposed to be some kind of middle ground? 

*laughs bitterly*

I don't even know what that would look like though. exact_reply:
*takes a deep breath, really sitting with the question*

I mean... it's everywhere, isn't it? But when I really think about it...

*counts on fingers*

My mom, obviously. Every sacrifice story. Every "I haven't read a book for pleasure in years" comment. The way she'd look at me sometimes like I was the reason her life got small.

But also... like, when I told my academic advisor I was pregnant, the first thing she said was "what are your plans?" Not congratulations, not how are you feeling - just this immediate assumption that I'd be dropping out. And when I said I wanted to stay enrolled, she gave me this look. Like... sympathetic but also kind of pitying. Like I was being naive.

*voice gets more animated*

And social media is the worst. Either you see these perfect Instagram moms who clearly have money and help and make it look effortless, or you see articles about how the first three years are critical for brain development and if you're not doing tummy time and reading to them constantly you're ruining them. There's no space for "mom who also has her own intellectual life."

*pause, touching belly again*

Even my friends have kind of... disappeared? Like they don't know what to say to me anymore. One of them actually said "well, your party days are over!" Like my whole identity is just going to be diapers and baby food now.

*looks at you directly*

I think the message is: you can be a mom OR a person. Not both. Especially not if you're young and single and poor.
